discussions.title: Bold Brew v2.2.0 - Update & New Features
discussions.body:
Hi Bluefin Team! 👋
This is not a bug report, just a quick update on Bold Brew developments that might interest you.
I've just released version [2.2.0](https://github.com/Valkyrie00/bold-brew/releases/tag/v2.2.0) with some features that could be useful for the Bluefin community:

--- 

**Remote Brewfile Support**
You can now load Brewfiles directly from remote:
```
# Local file (as before)
bbrew -f ~/my-tools.Brewfile

# Remote URL (new!)
bbrew -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/your-org/configs/main/dev-tools.Brewfile
```
This means you could create and share versioned, curated Brewfile collections for different use cases (IDE tools, AI/ML packages, DevOps tools, etc.) that users can load directly without downloading anything.
Examples:
```
bbrew -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ublue-os/homebrew-tap/refs/heads/main/ublue-os-artwork.Brewfile
bbrew -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ublue-os/homebrew-tap/refs/heads/main/ublue-os-ide.Brewfile
```

--- 

**Flatpak Support**
I've noted the interest in Flatpak support, it's on my roadmap and I'll be working on it in the coming period.

--- 

**One-liner Install Script (Beta)**
I've also created an all-in-one install script that installs Homebrew + Bold Brew in a single command:
```
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Valkyrie00/bold-brew/main/install.sh)"
```
It's still in beta and needs more testing, but it's a step toward making the initial setup faster for users.

I'll keep you updated on future developments. If there's anything specific you need or any feedback, please let me know!
Thanks for the continued support!  😃

discussions.title: Bluefin LTS testing branches now available
discussions.body:
Alright, as some of you have noticed Achillobator has been sick for the past two weeks. This was due to trying to deploy the testing branch in the LTS repo, which we tested and deployed back then but it never worked right despite working as intended during testing.  

After much gnashing of teeth by multiple people, ends up it was a misconfigured branch protection rule inside the github settings, our setup was right the entire time! Computers suck. 

(Thanks to @ahmedadan and @hanthor for bringing this one home!)

Run a bootc status to figure out what image you're on and tack a `-testing` at the end of the tag: 

    bootc switch --enforce-container-sigpolicy ghcr.io/ublue-os/bluefin-dx:lts-hwe-testing

... and so on. Note that the rebase helper has not implemented this yet. Ideally it'd just be one command to switch without figuring these out. But we wanted to get these fixes out asap. Things are running great on my machines, and the GNOME logout bug thing is fixed. We'll let these bake over the weekend (reports and issues always appreciated!) and likely push to prod on monday. 

We're using the [wei pull app](https://github.com/wei/pull) to manage this. You can [check the config here](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin-lts/blob/main/.github/pull.yml). This lets us merge things into the testing branch, which produces builds right away, then we can land them in the `lts` production branch. 

If you're on a custom image you may want to consider copying this config, it's really nice to rev in a testing branch. I've PRed it to the template so at some point everyone can just have this. 

Thanks for your patience!

discussions.title: Bluefin LTS is close to release candidate
discussions.body:
Ok now that we've figured out the kernel cadence we're pretty good to go with `bluefin:lts`. To catch everyone up we've revisited our kernel policy: https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin-lts/issues/576

That means that the vanilla `:lts` will just have a stock kernel and GNOME, and that wasn't a problem. James also freshened the ISOs up so you should be good to go: https://docs.projectbluefin.io/downloads/#anaconda-installer-downloads

We're still waiting to implement secure boot so that remains the biggest release blocker. But other than that it's basically finished. Give it a whirl and let us know! We still need to add ZFS and bazaar too. 

If you use an Nvidia card the GDX variant is still waiting on us to move over to the official Nvidia drivers with CUDA. 

That leaves the `bluefin-lts:testing` branch, which we will call `bluefin-lts:hwe`. This will have a full GNOME 48 backport, and will follow the same kernel cadence as the rest of Bluefin. We're going to move to a "latest version of the last minor kernel" approach as we're almost always running on a pinned kernel anyway these days. 

If this was live today we'd stay on `6.14.11` until the `6.16.0` kernel is released then we'll move to whatever the last `6.15.x` was. We can adjust this at any time.

discussions.title: Agent to help with supporting OS operation?
discussions.body:
I wondered if there is a plan to add a specialized LLM-based agent that can help with collecting issue information and suggest solutions. Obviously that would create many headaches in terms of permissions, but given the heterogeneity of hardware, it would be helpful to have such an agent on each desktop, at least an agent based on open-source models with a small footprint. I assume the ublue-os teams already use similar tools. Given the immutability of the system, the tasks that it could accomplish could be
- Receive a query for a specific issue with a system service/unit (why is DNS not working?).
- Receive general query (please collect issues that occurred on the latest boot).
- Query journalctl to collect helpful messages.
- Read system configuration under `/etc`.
- Search online forums such as Bugzilla or the ublue-os/bluefin GitHub discussion board and issues.
- Recommend edits to configuration files and configuration commands.
- In case the issue cannot be solved due to lack of permissions or complexity, it could ask permission to escalate by generating an issue on GitHub automatically with all the relevant information. These issues could have an #agent-generated tag to filter them out (or in) easily.

I noticed that certain log messages already provide such suggestions with a degree of certainty attached to them. Is this generated by similar tools? A more visible interface would be appreciated. It could potentially alleviate developer burden as well, as issues could be picked up automatically. It does have a lot of potential for unsafe behavior, though.

discussions.title: How to turn off the laptop screen?
discussions.body:
Hello everyone!

I understand my question might seem strange to most people, so let me quickly explain the context.

I am a completely blind Linux user, relying on the Orca screen reader with various speech synthesizers, such as RHVoice. I have been using Linux for over ten years now. Everything suggests that I will be installing Bluefin on my laptop soon, once the Stable F43 stream is released. My motivation is partly due to my beloved wife, who is also blind. Following the end of Windows 10 support on October 14th, she is considering switching to Linux, and she likes the concept of Fedora Silverblue. Since official Fedora Silverblue lacks a proper Live environment, Bluefin is our current choice.

In this regard, I have a question:

Could someone please advise on **how to reliably turn the laptop screen on and off**?

Sometimes, I need to reliably disable the display for privacy reasons.

I would be extremely grateful for an answer. AI tools like Gemini and ChatGPT suggest various options, but since I am completely blind, I cannot visually verify the result. I need a solution that is known to work 100% reliably on Bluefin.

Thank you very much.

discussions.title: Critique my backup strategy
discussions.body:
Hello, I'm a long-time Linux user, first time Bluefin user. I back up my computers with [borg](https://borgbackup.readthedocs.io/).

I want to do a complete backup of a laptop with Bluefin, in the sense that I can fully restore the state of the OS/user data if the laptop gets lost etc.

My thinking is that full system backups of immutable distros are probably pointless and counterproductive, so I came up with the following.

**Backup**

* Make btrfs snapshots of `/var` and `/var/home` (and any other manually-created btrfs subvolumes).
* Backup these snapshots and `/etc` (which is not a subvolume and cannot be snapshotted(?))

**Full restore**

* Install Bluefin
* Boot into the new installation
* Overwrite `/var`, `/var/home`, and `/etc` with the backup
* Reboot

**Questions**

* Does my backup contain _all_ mutable state?
* Will my restore work?

Thanks for any hints and tips.

discussions.title: One post-install gotcha: unprepared for Secure Boot enrollment password prompt
discussions.body:
I installed Bluefin tonight on a shiny new Framework 12. It was overall an easy process. It was my first installation of a RH/Fedora product since 2008!

I'm looking forward to experiencing this cool distro that @castrojo and co. have cooked up. I'm especially stoked that Homebrew is a first-class citizen on Bluefin.

One thing that caught me off guard during setup was a prompt for a password for Secure Boot enrollment during the first reboot after installation. I didn't see a screen during installation that warned about it, but was easily able to pull up the [secure boot section of the Installation Guide](https://docs.projectbluefin.io/installation/#secure-boot) to find the password.

It would be nice if the last screen before reboot provided some nudge for that password.

> After reboot, you'll be prompted for a password for Secure Boot enrollment. That password is `universalblue`. Write this down so that you don't forget it. If you do, you can find it in the installation guide.

That type of message would help. It would have saved me ~30-60 seconds but a good bit of friction in an otherwise smooth, polished installation process.

discussions.title: Facilitating donating to developers
discussions.body:
#### tl;dr:
The future of the Linux desktop depends on app developers having a monetary interest to build for and support Linux. I would like to give X amount of money every month to Linux apps I use and want to support. Right now this process is time consuming; a streamlined process could vastly increase the revenue for Linux app developers.

#### The Problem
This morning I was thinking that I want to stop free-loading and actually give back to some of the people that make my digital life a whole lot easier. 

This turned out to be  a lot more of a chore when I started to dig for donation links for the projects I want to support. While Bazaar makes this easier, there was still a looot of clicks. The annoyance of this would compound since I would like to donate regularly without going through this process every time.

#### The Dream
I assume I have some kindred spirits in this community when I say that I like to **automate everything**. My ideal process for donating to open source projects would be:

1. Input my payment information
2. Choose from a list of installed software what I want to donate to (brew packages, flatpaks, maybe custom paypal links for other stuff)
3. Choose how much I want to donate, either to individual apps or make a pool that distributes the donation evenly.

_Bonus_: option to have apps removed from the donation list if I haven't used them for some amount of time (or add apps that aren't on the list but I use frequently)

#### Obstacles

Disclaimer: I'm an engineer with a lot of experience programming, but I haven't dealt extensively with payment systems. 

From what I can tell, these are the main obstacles before one could even start to fix this problem through an app or some kind of system integration:

- **Non-standardized payment methods**. Flathub has the best donation support and does the best job in facilitating money going from users to Linux app developers. This is very unfortunate since their solution is so la...

discussions.title: Fresh Bluefin install in degraded mode: btrfs compress parameters not applied
discussions.body:
I noticed that a fresh Bluefin install is in `degraded` mode. 

```
❯ systemctl is-system-running 
degraded
```

```
❯ systemctl --failed
  UNIT                       LOAD   ACTIVE SUB    DESCRIPTION                         
● systemd-remount-fs.service loaded failed failed Remount Root and Kernel File Systems

Legend: LOAD   → Reflects whether the unit definition was properly loaded.
        ACTIVE → The high-level unit activation state, i.e. generalization of SUB.
        SUB    → The low-level unit activation state, values depend on unit type.

1 loaded units listed.
```

Looks like `/etc/fstab` parameters are ignored, and the btrfs filesystem is not mounted **without** compress parameters. Notice `compress=zstd:1` is present in fstab but not present in the output of the `mount` command.

I think this is an ostree upstream issue, but I couldn't find this referenced in the bug tracker. Is this known? There is a workaround discussed in the linked tickets and I wonder what is bluefin's stance on implementing it. 

https://discussion.fedoraproject.org/t/talk-mount-options-are-ignored-in-fedora-atomic-desktops-42/148874

https://gitlab.com/fedora/ostree/sig/-/issues/72

```
❯ cat /etc/fstab 

#
# /etc/fstab
# Created by anaconda on Sun Dec 28 13:10:16 2025
#
# Accessible filesystems, by reference, are maintained under '/dev/disk/'.
# See man pages fstab(5), findfs(8), mount(8) and/or blkid(8) for more info.
#
# After editing this file, run 'systemctl daemon-reload' to update systemd
# units generated from this file.
#
# Updated by bootc-fstab-edit.service
UUID=fe088622-bc6f-418b-ad73-9ae1b02268f1 / btrfs subvol=root,compress=zstd:1,ro 0 0
UUID=ac5591fb-fa14-4c5e-965f-cb53e183b6d4 /boot ext4 defaults 1 2
UUID=AE69-D9B7 /boot/efi vfat umask=0077,shortname=winnt 0 2
UUID=fe088622-bc6f-418b-ad73-9ae1b02268f1 /home btrfs subvol=home,compress=zstd:1 0 0
UUID=fe088622-bc6f-418b-ad73-9ae1b02268f1 /var btrfs subvol=var,compress=zstd...

discussions.title: Thoughts on a support tier system?
discussions.body:
One of the bummers of the current Bluefin design is that it's very binary. We optimize for the best experience but not edge cases outside of the model. Worst case you use Ubuntu or something but at least the computer is functioning!

However this leads to friction, and a recent talk at FOSDEM from Mike McQuaid (can someone find a link) talked about how they solved this in homebrew.

They have a tier system where the top tier is the intended and tested experience, and then this helps expectations. Something like:

- Tier 0 - The intended experience, linux friendly hardware, no layering, etc.
- Tier 1 - You have to layer a thing or two because VPNs. We'd include other things that aren't dealbreakers but are on the way out. 
- Tier 3 - you need something janky. This is more of a "Look I got it working" flex. We all had that one laptop ... 

That way it becomes more clear where you are in the chain, when you're on Tier 1 you should understand that upgrades are liable to break and that you should pay attention, things like that.

discussions.title: Feature request: Include nix in Bluefin-DX
discussions.body:
Hello,

Opening this as a discussion though it is more of a feature request.

I know that Bluefin has a special relationship with Homebrew, and I do use homebrew a lot, but there is a steady growing following of Nix as a package manager for developer environments. A lot of people (including myself) build [custom images to include nix](https://github.com/karypid/bluenix)

As a testament to this growing popularity, FESCo recently approved the use of the root folder - [media article here](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Fedora-Nix-Dir-Approved) - and already [a package is prepared and approved](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2388768#c76).

I don't consider Nix and Homebrew as competing solutions. If Bluefin-DX included this new Nix package by default a lot of developers using it would stop building custom images.

Could you just add this new RPM to DX by default and help make the lives of Bluefin users that work on projects with nix-packaged development environments easy?

GNOME has been crashing on me a fair bit lately; it seems to have something to do with Chrome, but not exactly sure. Based on the logs, the cause of the GNOME crash was a graphics driver failure

   1. Root Cause: The amdgpu kernel driver experienced a hardware timeout (ring
      gfx_0.0.0 timeout). It attempted to reset the GPU to recover, but this reset
      failed.
   2. Chain Reaction:
       * Chrome Crashed (`10:06:00`): Google Chrome, which was using the GPU,

       * GNOME Shell Crashed (`10:06:00`): The desktop environment (gnome-shell)

discussions.title: [FYI] Update your `mandb` with homebrew manpages!
discussions.body:
I didn't realize that `/usr/sbin/mandb` doesn't add any of the manpages installed by homebrew to the `whatis`/`apropos` database until today, when I started wondering why the man pages for tools like `shellcheck` that I have installed via brew aren't offered as completions in the Emacs `man` command.

You can check if this is the case on your system like this:
```
$ apropos brew
brew: nothing appropriate.
```

If you see "nothing appropriate" instead of this:
```
$ apropos brew
brew (1)             - The Missing Package Manager for macOS (or Linux)
```

then your whatis database is outdated and you probably have thousands of manpages that aren't part of the index. This command will add all of the missing manpages to the database:

```bash
$ mandb "$(brew --prefix)/share/man"
```

This added 17300 man pages to the DB on my system!

Now, rather than remembering to do this manually, we can let `systemd` do it for us:

`~/.config/systemd/user/homebrew-mandb.service`:
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Update Homebrew mandb database

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c 'mandb "$(brew --prefix)/share/man"'
```
`~/.config/systemd/user/homebrew-mandb.timer`:
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Run Homebrew mandb weekly

[Timer]
OnCalendar=weekly
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

and then in the shell:
```
$ systemctl --user daemon-reload
$ systemctl --user enable homebrew-mandb.timer
$ systemctl --user start homebrew-mandb.timer
```

and Bob's your uncle.

discussions.title: Recent battery life improvements?
discussions.body:
Is anyone else seeing significant battery life improvements recently? I think this bug fix might explain why:

https://github.com/kolunmi/bazaar/issues/237

Recently, I came to the conclusion that my battery was failing so I ordered a replacement.

Earlier this week the battery arrived and I did a before and after test. First, I ran a full discharge test on the old battery. Then I repeated the same test with the new one:

| Battery      | Capacity (Wh) | Runtime (hh:mm) |
| ------------ | ------------- | --------------- |
| Old battery  | 55 Wh         | 9h 28m          |
| New battery  | 61 Wh         | 11h 50m         |

For each test the computer was completely idle. It was just sitting at an empty desktop. The screen was on, Wi-Fi was on, and keyboard back-light off.

These results are far better than expected!

Previously, I thought my battery was failing because when I upgraded my Framework from the Intel 1280P mainboard to the AMD HX 370, I was pretty disappointed with battery life. After the upgrade, I was only getting 1–4 hours, and there was one time when the laptop died after just 1–2 hours while merely displaying PDFs and basic web pages full of text.

discussions.title: Adding an emoji picker
discussions.body:
### Proposal: Add a dedicated emoji picker to improve UX

I use emojis a lot, both when writing messages (Slack, chat apps, etc.) and when developing websites. Right now on Bluefin, the way GNOME handles emojis through the **Characters** app feels pretty lackluster and inconvenient.  

I think it would add some nice polish to the overall UX—especially for users new to Linux—if Bluefin shipped with a **dedicated emoji picker**, bound to a shortcut like `Super + .` (matching Windows/KDE Plasma)

Two good options that are already **Flatpak-first** and integrate well with GNOME are:  
- [**Smile**](https://mijorus.it/projects/smile/)  
- [**Emote**](https://github.com/tom-james-watson/Emote)  

Both are lightweight, work seamlessly with GTK apps, and would provide a more intuitive experience than Characters.

I realize this would add another default app, but I think the usability benefits outweigh the cost. Having a consistent emoji shortcut would make Bluefin feel more polished and welcoming, especially for new users migrating from other platforms.

**Open question:** Would the team be open to including one of these tools by default, or do you see a different approach to solving emoji input UX on Bluefin?

discussions.title: Disaster recovery Drill with Bluefin
discussions.body:
Today I ran a full disaster recovery drill to see whether I could restore my laptop from backups without access to the original machine.

I'm impressed by how well it went.

It took me ~60-90 min to recover in a VM. In that time I was able to re-install Bluefin, enable dev mode, restore my home directory with Pika Backup, reinstall all my applications (via `brew bundle --file ~/.Brewfile`), and layer 1Password.

At that point, I was productive again. I could open my work projects, build the dev containers, and run the software.

Beyond that I found some minor gotchas

- In gnome, all my previous extensions were installed, but I needed to re-enable them by clicking the toggle. (I use the Caffeine extension, and Copyous extension).
- Most apps worked flawlessly. But I needed to restart 1password using a peculiar procedure in order to get the main GUI to appear.
- Machine-specific items (Tailscale login, Bluetooth pairing) naturally required reconfiguration.

None of these blocked productivity.

My advice to other Bluefin users is to: 
- dump your `.Brewfile` automatically. I wrote a tiny systemd service + timer that runs `brew bundle dump --global --force --describe` everyday at 1AM. This updates my `~/.Brewfile` so I can recover my applications quickly.
- backup automatically :smile:. I use Deja Dup for cloud backups and Pika Backup for local backups. I highly recommend either or both.

**Conclusion**
Bluefin is something special. I’ve been using Bluefin since March 2025, and this drill helped me assess my backup and recovery story.

- The procedure was pretty easy.
- The replacement system felt “lived in” immediately. 
- Every time I opened any applications, it picked up right where I left off.
- All my files were exactly where they were supposed to be (including dotfiles)
- all my systemd user services came back automatically.

Really impressive experience overall. 👍

So if you're using Bluefin, you can probably get by with a backup o...

discussions.title: Difficulties Due to Invisible System Changes
discussions.body:
I need to get this off my chest.

I’m a huge fan of Universal Blue and generally very happy with it. But what really frustrates me is how hard it can be to understand what’s happening under the hood. For example, Atuin recently stopped working, simply because an entry in my .bashrc had vanished.

So here’s the problem: Did the devs clean up the .bashrc? Why was it removed?

Figuring this out is a nightmare. The only way is to actively dig into what changes the OS is making – I literally have to search for it myself.

And it was just as annoying with the ujust cleanup. I get why it exists, but if I wasn’t paying close attention, I would have completely missed the changes and ended up thinking: “Wait, what happened? Where did this go?”

I would really, really appreciate a simple system update history. It doesn’t need to track everything, just the changes that affect important configurations. Even a small popup like “ujust cleaned up, entries moved” would be enough.

It would make it so much easier to figure out whether the problem is on my end or if the system has simply changed.

discussions.title: Why does my bluefin server queries all available dns servers for its own hostname every 30 seconds?
discussions.body:
Every 30 seconds, my Bluefin  server queries all available dns servers for its own hostname, "bluefin". The number of queries all sent at once varies (usually 1 to 8 queries per server), but the repetition is roughly every 30 seconds. The response is always NXDOMAIN (unless I add a redirect ; see below).

On my Bluefin server, I have an AdGuard Home dns server running in Docker with an upstream Unbound server, also in Docker. I also have an AdGuard Home dns server running running as an add-on in Home Assistant on a separate machine. Both accept queries from LAN and over Tailscale.

The queries continue :
-  if I change the upstream server from Unbound to 1.1.1.1
- if I stop AdGuard Home on my Bluefin server (the queries keep coming to  AdGuard Home  in Home Assistant)
- if I stop all docker containers on my Bluefin server
- if I disable Tailscale
- if I add the IP of bluefin to /etc/hosts
- if I add a redirect to the IP of bluefin in one or both AdGuard servers
- if I disable avahi-daemon

I can live with those queries, but I have gone down tis rabbit hole and now I *really* want to know where they come from, what their purpose is, if they are the symptom of an actual issue, etc. 

And it would be nice if they were not flooding my AdGuard Home servers, of course!

discussions.title: The naming scheme for releases is quite unintuitive
discussions.body:
I know it is not thta much of a big deal, as they can be rebased, but the current release naming scheme is pretty confusing.

Bluefin LTS - that's fair enough. LTS is widely understood, I think?
Bluefin GTS - this is a nightmare. In Europe at least, GTS badges were placed on cars which were released with "sports" trim. The implication is that Bluefin GTS is a souped up version of Bluefin for those who like to show off, drive fast and are not afraid of accidents. This seems to be the opposite of what you're trying to present on the GTS stream?
Bluefin (aka Bluefin Stable) - there's too much baggage in the Linux world around the word "stable". Debian has the mindshare with that. It implies something which has been in testing for years before release, rather than something which is factory fresh. (I know you present this as `Bluefin` rather than `Bluefin Stable` on the download page but that's not much better as the single word moniker also implies it should be the default.)

I'd propose:
Bluefin LTS - as is
Bluefin - to replace Bluefin GTS
Bluefin Weekly - to replace Bluefin (Stable)
Bluefine Daily - to replace stable-daily

Thoughts?

discussions.title: Error Attempting to Update Bluefin
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Greeting -->
Hi @therealknewman! I'm [Dosu](https://go.dosu.dev/dosubot) and I’m helping the bluefin team.

<!-- Answer -->
This is a [known issue](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/issues/4197) caused by a previous signing key rotation fix that modified your `/etc/containers/policy.json` to point to a path that no longer exists. The signing keys have since been relocated.

**To fix it**, restore the correct policy file from the image:

```bash
sudo cp /usr/etc/containers/policy.json /etc/containers/policy.json
```

After that, run `ujust update` again and you should be good to go.

**Why this happened:** Back in July 2024, a fix script modified your policy.json to point to `/etc/pki/containers/ublue-os.pub`. Later, the keys were moved to `/usr/lib/pki/containers/`, but ostree doesn't auto-merge changes to locally modified files—leaving your system pointing to a non-existent location.

For more info, see the [Bluefin documentation](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).

discussions.answer_body:
Figured it out, not sure if this is the best way, but it worked.  Here is what i did:

copied cert to /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/

discussions.body:
I’m trying to get a custom Root CA trusted system-wide on Bluefin, but CLI apps like `curl` and `openssl` won’t validate my internal server, even though browsers work when I manually add the cert.

1. Placed root CA in `/etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/` and ran `sudo update-ca-trust extract`.
2. Verified root CA is in `/etc/pki/ca-trust/extracted/pem/tls-ca-bundle.pem`.

Bluefin seems stricter than other servers I’ve used — installing just the root CA doesn’t make CLI apps trust the server.

What’s the recommended way to install a custom root CA on Bluefin so that CLI apps like `curl` and `openssl` trust internal servers? Are there any Bluefin-specific steps I’m missing?

discussions.title: How to automatically update Brew packages
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Greeting -->
Hey @jamesericdavidson! 👋 I'm here to help you with bugs, questions, and more while you wait for a human maintainer.

<!-- Answer -->
Yes, Bluefin provides a method to automatically update Brew packages through a systemd service. The `brew-update.service` is designed for this purpose. It sets specific environment variables necessary for Homebrew and runs as the user with UID 1000 by default. The service is triggered by a systemd timer, which starts 10 minutes after the system boots and then every 6 hours thereafter <sup>[[1]](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)</sup><sup>[[2]](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/blob/main/system_files/shared/usr/lib/systemd/system/brew-update.service)</sup><sup>[[3]](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/blob/main/system_files/shared/usr/lib/systemd/system/brew-update.timer)</sup>.

For more details, you can refer to the official documentation at [https://docs.projectbluefin.io](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).

discussions.title: How do stable images iherit flavors (main, nvidia, nvidia-open)?
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Answer -->
Stable images inherit all flavors (main, nvidia, nvidia-open) because the stable workflow calls the reusable-build.yml workflow, which sets a default for image_flavors as ['main', 'nvidia', 'nvidia-open'] if not explicitly provided. So, unless overridden, all three flavors are always built for stable by default. The explicit declaration in the latest workflow is only needed if you want a different set of flavors; otherwise, it's just repeating the default. The generate-release.yml workflow only handles release tagging and doesn't affect flavor iteration. You can see this default in the reusable-build.yml file [here](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/blob/6c15552141c912b17ea6dcab11d75512a1ac49a4/.github/workflows/reusable-build.yml).

For more details, check the [official documentation](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).

Tip: You can use tldr pages (e.g., `tldr jq`) to quickly learn about CLI tools used in these workflows!

discussions.title: How do I pin my current image to a specific date?
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Answer -->
To pin your current image to a specific date, you can use the following command:

```bash
sudo bootc switch ghcr.io/ublue-os/bluefin:stable-20241027 --enforce-container-sigpolicy
```

This command switches to the image tagged with the specific date `20241027`. If you are using an NVIDIA machine, make sure to include `-nvidia` in the image name:

```bash
sudo bootc switch ghcr.io/ublue-os/bluefin-nvidia:stable --enforce-container-sigpolicy
```

Additionally, you can use the `ublue-rebase-helper` script to assist with rebasing to a specific date. This script allows you to list available tags and choose a specific date to rebase to, providing a user-friendly way to manage image versions <sup>[[1]](https://docs.projectbluefin.io/administration)</sup><sup>[[2]](https://github.com/ublue-os/packages/blob/main/packages/ublue-rebase-helper/src/ublue-rebase-helper)</sup>.

For more details, you can refer to the official documentation at [https://docs.projectbluefin.io](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).

discussions.title: How do I swap out wpa-supplicant with iwd?
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Answer -->
To switch from wpa-supplicant to iwd on Bluefin, use standard systemd commands: disable the wpa_supplicant service and enable the iwd service. For example:

```sh
sudo systemctl disable --now wpa_supplicant
sudo systemctl enable --now iwd
```

If you're using NetworkManager, it will automatically use iwd if wpa_supplicant is not running and iwd is available. There are no Bluefin-specific steps or automation for this process—just follow conventional Linux practice. You can confirm iwd is included in Bluefin's package list [here](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/blob/21e50c7fe971deeb39569362772f864dab0263e8/packages.json).

Tip: Use `tldr systemctl` for a quick guide on managing services.

For more on Bluefin, see the [official documentation](https://docs.projectbluefin.io) (note: there are currently no iwd-specific instructions).

discussions.title: How to rebase from `bluefin:stable` to `bluefin:gts` after `gts` is on Fedora 42?
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Answer -->
To rebase from `bluefin:stable` to `bluefin:gts` after GTS is on Fedora 42, use the following steps:

1. Get your image name:
   ```sh
   IMAGE_NAME=$(jq -r '.["image-name"]' < /usr/share/ublue-os/image-info.json)
   ```
2. Rebase to the GTS stream:
   ```sh
   sudo bootc switch --enforce-container-sigpolicy ghcr.io/ublue-os/$IMAGE_NAME:gts
   ```
   To rebase to a specific GTS release (for example, a dated tag):
   ```sh
   sudo bootc switch --enforce-container-sigpolicy ghcr.io/ublue-os/$IMAGE_NAME:gts-20250803
   ```
   [Reference](https://docs.projectbluefin.io/changelogs/bluefin-gts-20250803#how-to-rebase)

**Caveats & Solutions:**
- Package changes: Some packages may be replaced with Flatpaks or Homebrew installs in Fedora 42. After rebasing, run:
  ```sh
  ujust install-system-flatpaks
  ```
  This will ensure your applications are updated or replaced as needed. Review the [Fedora 42 package changes](https://universal-blue.discourse.group/t/upcoming-changes-to-bluefin-packages-for-the-f42-release/7711/1) for details.
- Customizations: If you have custom configurations or installed packages, check the [changelogs](https://docs.projectbluefin.io/changelogs/tags/gts) for any breaking changes or removals.

**Tracking Fedora Versions for Each Tag:**
- The `stable` tag always tracks the current Fedora release, while `gts` tracks the previous stable Fedora version. You can confirm which Fedora version each tag is on by checking the [Bluefin release notes](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/releases), the [official documentation](https://docs.projectbluefin.io/administration), or the [Universal Blue discourse group](https://universal-blue.discourse.group/) for announcements and changelogs.

For more details and the latest instructions, always refer to the [official Bluefin documentation](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).  
Tip: Use `tldr bootc` for a quick summary of bootc commands.

discussions.title: Using GPG-Smartcards and YubiKey on bluefin
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Answer -->
Smartcard support on Bluefin is mostly handled by upstream defaults—there are no custom system-level tweaks for legacy devices like the Crypto Stick. If gpg works with your YubiKey but not the Crypto Stick, it's likely a compatibility issue with the drivers or middleware, not a Bluefin-specific block. 

A few things that have helped other users: try restarting the `pcscd` service after plugging in your token, and consider removing the `opensc` package if it's present, as it can interfere with smartcard detection ([details here](https://universal-blue.discourse.group/t/gpg-smartcard-support-with-usb-security-keys/397/9)). Also, check for warnings about outdated `gpg-agent` or `scdaemon` versions—restarting them with `gpgconf --kill all` can sometimes help ([see this thread](https://universal-blue.discourse.group/t/gpg-smartcard-support-with-usb-security-keys/397)).

For the `ssh-add -K` error, make sure your PKCS#11 provider is correctly configured and that you're using the system's built-in smartcard tools, not Homebrew versions, to avoid service conflicts ([more info](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/issues/2229)).

For more details, check the [official Bluefin documentation](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).

**tldr tip:**  
To restart all GPG-related services, run:  
```sh
gpgconf --kill all
sudo systemctl restart pcscd
```

discussions.title: How to discern installation in UEFI boot
discussions.answer_body:
<!-- Answer -->
All Universal Blue OSes (Aurora, Bluefin, Bazzite) use the default 'fedora' UEFI boot entry name, so if you install them on separate SSDs, your UEFI boot menu will show multiple entries labeled 'fedora'—they won't be automatically distinguishable by name ([source](https://github.com/ublue-os/bluefin/blob/21e50c7fe971deeb39569362772f864dab0263e8/iso_files/configure_iso_anaconda.sh)). 

To make them easier to tell apart, you can manually rename the boot entries after installation using efibootmgr. For example:
```sh
sudo efibootmgr -b 0001 -L "Bluefin"
```
Replace `0001` with the actual boot entry number for the OS you want to rename (find these with `efibootmgr -v`).

tldr tip:  
`tldr efibootmgr` gives you quick usage examples for this tool.

For more on system management, check out the [official Bluefin docs](https://docs.projectbluefin.io).

discussions.title: Cannot mount Google Drive in Nautilus
discussions.answer_body: I fixed by removing Google authentication in Gnome Settings and reauthorizing it. Also installed gnome-online-accounts in bbrew,  but not sure if that was required. bbrew indicated package was not installed.
discussions.body:
When I try to access Google Drive from Nautilus, I get a dialogue screen saying Permission Denied. I've successfully added the Google account through Settings>Online Accounts. Firefox opened and after checking the boxes to add Google Drive access the screen appears to complete.  I can access Google drive through the web browser without issue. Microsoft OneDrive mounts without issue.

I think the Permission Denied must be referring to a file in the OS, perhaps a mount point? Not finding any discussion of this issue in Nautilus, Gnome or Fedora. I tried using RClone to mount the Google Drive and did so successfully, however it was a "bad" mount in that some Drive files did not function properly. This is a known issue in RClone. https://rclone.org/drive/#limitations-of-google-docs. I see other Fedora users getting a mount in Nautilus. Thanks for your ideas on resolving this.
